skill.name: github-workflow-automation
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through GitHub Actions automation, CI/CD workflow design, PR coordination, release tasks, and repository operations.
skill.summary: Automate GitHub Actions workflows and repository coordination with AI-assisted CI/CD guidance.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, filesystem, and secret-related patterns in SKILL.md. AI review found no prompt-injection attempt or confirmed malicious code, but the skill remains medium risk because its examples guide users to run third-party GitHub automation commands that can mutate repositories and use workflow secrets.

medium_findings.title: Executable GitHub Automation Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill contains many npx, gh, npm, mkdir, and shell pipeline examples. They are documentation snippets, not bundled executable code, but users who run them can install third-party packages, modify workflows, create issues, and change repository state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in fenced examples and setup instructions. They are legitimate for a GitHub automation skill, but they invoke external tools and mutate repository or GitHub state when copied and run.

medium_findings.title: Workflow Examples Reference Secrets and OIDC Permissions
medium_findings.description: The skill shows GitHub Actions examples that pass secrets into commands and request OIDC token permissions. This is normal for deployment automation, but poor copy-paste usage could expose credentials or over-grant workflow permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly reference GitHub secrets, API_KEY, and id-token permission. The surrounding section frames them as security best practices, so this is a controlled operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Unvalidated Workflow Input in Shell Example
medium_findings.description: A reusable workflow example passes a workflow input directly into an npx command argument. This is a common Actions pattern, but users should validate inputs before adopting the pattern in privileged workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line clearly interpolates a GitHub Actions input into a shell command. The example is not automatically executed by the skill, so the risk depends on user adoption and workflow permissions.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive-File and Obfuscation Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Windows SAM and obfuscation alerts are caused by shell variables, jq processing, and base64 decoding inside a security issue parsing example. No evidence found of attempts to read Windows credential databases or hide malicious payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code is a GitHub Actions YAML example that parses scanner output and creates GitHub issues. The suspicious terms come from shell syntax and encoding used for line-safe parsing, not credential database access.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory and Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Benign Documentation
low_findings.description: The hidden file alerts point to ~/.npm cache paths, and the hardcoded URLs point to public documentation and project links. No evidence found of hidden credential access or network exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are cache configuration and public support links. They do not contain commands that collect files or send local data to those URLs.

dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many npx, gh, npm, mkdir, and shell pipeline examples. They are documentation snippets, not bundled executable code, but users who run them can install third-party packages, modify workflows, create issues, and change repository state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in fenced examples and setup instructions. They are legitimate for a GitHub automation skill, but they invoke external tools and mutate repository or GitHub state when copied and run.

dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows GitHub Actions examples that pass secrets into commands and request OIDC token permissions. This is normal for deployment automation, but poor copy-paste usage could expose credentials or over-grant workflow permissions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly reference GitHub secrets, API_KEY, and id-token permission. The surrounding section frames them as security best practices, so this is a controlled operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.description: A reusable workflow example passes a workflow input directly into an npx command argument. This is a common Actions pattern, but users should validate inputs before adopting the pattern in privileged workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line clearly interpolates a GitHub Actions input into a shell command. The example is not automatically executed by the skill, so the risk depends on user adoption and workflow permissions.

content.value_statement: Manual GitHub workflow setup can be slow and inconsistent across repositories. This skill provides structured prompts and examples for CI/CD, PR checks, releases, and workflow optimization.
actual_capabilities: Drafts GitHub Actions workflow templates for CI, security scans, deployments, and releases.
Guides PR validation using GitHub CLI commands and multi-step review workflows.
Suggests workflow optimization patterns for caching, parallel jobs, and failure analysis.
Provides setup checklists for GitHub CLI, git, Node.js, and workflow directories.
Documents swarm-style coordination patterns for repository, issue, release, and CI tasks.
limitations: Does not include executable scripts beyond documentation examples in SKILL.md.
Requires users to install and trust external CLI tools before running examples.
Generated workflows must be reviewed for repository permissions and secret handling.
Some referenced commands depend on third-party packages and may change over time.

use_cases.title: Create CI Workflows
use_cases.description: Generate a starting GitHub Actions workflow for builds, tests, security checks, and caching.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Pull Request Validation
use_cases.description: Plan automated PR checks, review comments, labels, and failure analysis with GitHub CLI workflows.

use_cases.title: Improve Release Automation
use_cases.description: Design release, deployment, and rollback workflows with clearer sequencing and safer permission choices.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Basic Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a repository without CI.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to draft a basic GitHub Actions CI workflow for my repository. Include checkout, install, test, cache, and artifact steps.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize an Existing Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate cleanup for slow or duplicated workflows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current GitHub Actions workflow and suggest safer caching, parallel jobs, timeouts, and permissions. Explain each recommended change.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Pull Request Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced PR validation and reviewer coordination.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a PR validation process using GitHub Actions and GitHub CLI. Include test selection, security checks, comments, labels, and failure handling.

prompt_templates.title: Design Release Orchestration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced release and deployment workflow design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a release automation plan for a multi-service repository. Include versioning, changelog generation, deployment gates, rollback checks, and required secrets.

output_examples.input: Create a CI workflow for a Node.js project with tests and caching.
output: A workflow outline with checkout, Node setup, dependency cache, install, test, and artifact steps.
A short review note describing required permissions and secrets.

output_examples.input: Improve my slow pull request workflow.
output: A prioritized list of bottlenecks such as serial jobs, missing cache keys, and broad triggers.
Suggested workflow changes with risk notes for each automation step.

output_examples.input: Plan a release workflow for tagged versions.
output: A release sequence covering validation, changelog creation, artifact publishing, deployment approval, and rollback planning.

best_practices: Review every generated workflow before committing it to .github/workflows.
Use least-privilege GitHub token permissions and scoped repository secrets.
Pin third-party actions and verify CLI package sources before running commands.
anti_patterns: Do not copy commands into production repositories without checking permissions and side effects.
Do not pass unvalidated workflow inputs into privileged shell commands.
Do not grant broad secrets or OIDC permissions to workflows that do not need them.

faq.question: Does this skill run GitHub Actions commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. The skill provides instructions and examples. Users decide which commands or workflows to run.

faq.question: Which tools does it expect?
faq.answer: It references GitHub CLI, git, Node.js, claude-flow, and ruv-swarm command examples.

faq.question: Can it create production workflows?
faq.answer: It can draft production-oriented workflows, but users must review permissions, secrets, and third-party dependencies.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The examples can mutate repositories, install packages, and use secrets if copied into a workflow.

faq.question: Does it contain prompt injection text?
faq.answer: No evidence found during review of SKILL.md for prompt-injection phrases or override instructions.

faq.question: Is it suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
